item.title: soul sucking hunk of metal...
item.content:encoded:
The first time I read this, I was thoroughly unnerved. I have enough trouble keeping myself motivated and focused without the threat of some soul-destroying (or at least, silver cord-disconnecting) stele. I would've upvoted immediately, but I didn't think of it, and later when I found myself thinking of this again (normally if a skip is memorable enough for this to happen, well, that's one of my criteria for whether I upvote), I couldn't remember the numerical designation. Took me joining the IRC today and asking a few questions for me to find it again.
All that to say, I like this. It creeps me the hell out, but I like it, and if you could figure out what to do about the issue brought up by Hornby on page one of this discussion (I think those concerns are still relevant and the article still needs work to address them), I'd feel a lot better about my upvote! :-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this quite a lot! Rich and imaginative with a lot of potential for further lore expansion.

I have question, though; are the Homo sapiens descensus implied to be, or related to, SCP-1000? The physical description and technological advancements, along with their co-habitation with human ancestors are not dissimilar to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to ask the same question on destruction of the artifact. If it's possible for it to cause an EK-Class scenario, and serves no greater purpose, is perfectly understood, and isn't indestructible, why not neutralize the object?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A very good — and very educational! — response.

When I said "the dictator's fault," I was using that as shorthand for "the dictator and those others who enable the system," but clearly that's not a shorthand I should've used. (I agree that the situation is much larger than the Kim family itself, though I never managed to put two and two together to quite the extent you have.) The context of my objection was that while the issues are clearly systemic, it's still a system built and perpetuated by people, and those people must still be considered accountable for its perpetuation.

But as I said earlier, speaking at length on this just makes my mild hangup sound larger than it is — and I think your reply makes it clear that you've put more thought into the relevant real-world matters than I have anyway.

Good hearing from you, mate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the horrifying things about North Korea is that it is unclear how much of it is the dictator's fault, at least after Kim Il Sung. His son (especially) and grandson took relatively little interest in policymaking — in a view that I find very persuasive, North Korea is less a dictatorship and more a democracy with one voter where parties of elites compete for the favor of an essentially disinterested and uninvolved leader who, because his cult of personality is the fount of state legitimacy, cannot be unseated.

By the accounts of his boarding-school classmates in Switzerland, Kim Jong Un was a perfectly normal (if quiet) kid with an interest in basketball. No pulling the wings off of grasshoppers or torturing animals. No imperious treatment of his classmates. No signs of real sociopathy, either natural or induced by circumstances. The idea that the logic of the system itself produces sociopaths rather than nature or even nurture is more horrifying, because it implies that it is self-sustaining until the {Dear, Great} Leader is self-destructive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm also bothered by how easily this can be read as dehumanizing, but there's something else that bothers me too. Someone said, a few years ago, that one of the more important measures of a story like this is whether it absolves real people of their atrocities. That user didn't think this article did so, but…

+ Well...
- I can't agree.

When you say, as the Ethics Committee dissent does, that the Foundation is propping up a real-world dictatorship that exists to this day, what you're really saying is that the horrible things that must need to be done to keep the dictatorship running — that is, the horrible things that are being done — aren't all the dictator's fault. If the North Korean government needs Foundation assistance, or even if the artifact itself is making things so much easier for them, then they're not nearly awful enough to get the job done themselves. (But of course, in the real world, they are.)

I'm struggling to articulate why that bothers me both as a reader and a(n aspiring) writer. One could say that this story isn't set in the real world but rather a lookalike, and that this should exempt it from having to portray North Korea realistically. However, a story set in the real world doesn't actually exist in the real world; it also exists in a lookalike world, and the differences between that world and the real world unavoidably convey some sentiment, whether that sentiment is "Gunning down Nazis is heroic" or "Meeting and killing your clone in a Foundationverse hotel room would be scary."

There is no difference between a story set in the real world and a story set in a lookalike. They're both "What if reality, but X?" Call of Duty used to ask, "What if World War II, but you can be a hero and kill the Nazis?" This site asks "What if prison, but with evil statues and blinking is deadly?"

"What if North Korea, but the dictator isn't fully at fault for these conditions? What if North Korea, but to some extent, someone made their rulers and officials do all this?" where that someone or something is a fiction — a moral black hole. Of course I know the author doesn't believe these "what ifs" or the ideas behind them, and regardless of their intent I don't think that the article is propaganda serving that purpose. Still, these are among the ideas that the article brings to my mind.

I'm reminded of an old (now deleted?) Tale which portrayed the Holocaust as a well-intentioned containment effort for a virus. I remember mentioning bits of the Gerstein Report in its talk page, years ago — how the Nazis, clearly unafraid of any contagion, enthusiastically looted the dead. There was a definite factual error, there — historical inaccuracy undermining historical fiction — but factual inaccuracy wasn't my objection.

This article isn't nearly that bad. It even attempts to self-correct, with the Ethics Committee dissent. Explaining all my little insecurities magnifies them. Still, I feel I should -1 this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know what to tell you other than that the views expressed about the inevitability of democratic liberation, and about the special horror of totalitarian control, are really common among liberal democratic theorists. They're wrong, in my opinion, but they're not uncommon, especially among the sort of greyface ex-security-state folks the Foundation tends to hire.

I mean, the position "North Korea is uniquely bad, and democracy is an inevitable stage of human evolution" is basically just Francis Fukuyama in a nutshell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From my understanding of the piece, the Foundation has been artificially preventing the collapse of the DPRK government, which would have otherwise stopped being a highly stratified kleptocratic monarchy long-ago due to non-anomalous geopolitics. The Ethics Committee dissent isn't so much that they should go the other way and topple the government, but that they should stop preventing it from collapsing by itself - because at this point the benefits of keeping it afloat don't outweigh the human rights costs that the DPRK citizens are paying.

None of their criticisms seem to touch on the idea that the Foundation might have a warped view that prevents them from criticizing the many, many other repressive nations that exist in the world, or the repressive subsets that exist even within the "free" nations, or even the massive repression the Foundation itself carries out.

The moral dilemma posed in the article is whether or not the Foundation should stop taking action rather than start. Unless the Foundation is actively preventing the fall of other repressive governments/institutions elsewhere in the world, their continued existence has no moral bearing on the Foundation's actions in the DPRK. While I agree that some of the language of the piece is questionable and easily misconstrued, I'm not sure that

"wow north korea is worse than any other nation on earth and their government should be toppled"

is a valid takeaway from the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand where you’re coming from, and the discussion above is terrific and sensible. I just wanted to share my amusement that it took me three tries to interpret your point correctly.

My first two attempts involved:

  • assuming you were commenting on the number of upvotes as wiki groupthink in the cult of ophite (of which I am a card-carrying member)
  • thinking your “right now” was to remind the (primarily American) readership of their own authoritarian submissive personality traits. I mean, the Washington monument is a stele too, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That doesn't really affect the problems I noted. It doesn't really make sense in that case that the Ethics Committee of all people would fail to mention these things solely because the fall of the Berlin wall was recent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. This reads very differently in 2018 than 2012, when I wrote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Check the date on the Ethics Committee dissent. It was dated expressly so that the fall of the Berlin Wall, in recent history, would have informed their thinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm crying, that quote is too powerful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Ethics dissent doesn't mention anything about mass incarceration being authoritarian, unless I'm missing something. It only mentions that the Foundation could induce an authoritarian situation with D-Class as subjects. While it implies that D-Class might deserve it, it doesn't seem to be implied that D-Class might be, you know, already conditioned to live in an authoritarian regime, since that's what they've been doing.

The thing I take the most issue with here is the idea that North Korea is somehow one of the the last bastions of authoritarianism in our world. This isn't an idea that is discounted by the ethics review. In fact, it's the ethics review that specifically states it: "As recent events in the USSR and elsewhere have demonstrated, totalitarian dictatorship is not a necessary feature of human society."

None of their criticisms seem to touch on the idea that the Foundation might have a warped view that prevents them from criticizing the many, many other repressive nations that exist in the world, or the repressive subsets that exist even within the "free" nations, or even the massive repression the Foundation itself carries out. Maybe this is all intended to be behind the scenes in a meta-meta-level of interpretation, but it does very much seem (and I in no way mean to imply this was intentional on your part) like the focus is solely on "wow north korea is worse than any other nation on earth and their government should be toppled", which for the reasons Scantron outlined is kind of a dangerous message to be present here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Trust me, I'm an anarchist, and I have no sympathy for the DPRK as a government, or for their overall form of government.

Now, you're absolutely correct in specifying my post as an uncharitable interpretation. Had I defaulted to a charitable interpretation (and thus defaulted to silence, having nothing of interest to say), you wouldn't have bothered to elucidate your reasoning, and this conversation wouldn't be happening. It's genuinely nice to get confirmation that you didn't write it this way because you actually believed what it sounded like you did, and I really hope that the article could be changed to address this in a way that makes it clear — Hippo's commentary below does a much better job of this than I could, I think.

Even discounting the practical outcomes, I don't think defaulting to a charitable interpretation would necessarily have been wise. The POTUS is clearly itching for a genocidal nuclear war with North Korea, the military-industrial complex has his back, the media is more than ready to lend their tacit support, and much of the American populace express casual racist attitudes about North Korea that make all this possible. I feel much more comfortable being somewhat overzealous in defending the humanity of North Koreans than potentially failing to defend it enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has almost nothing to do with what I said. I can only guess at what you read into my post, but whatever it is, it is entirely not my problem. As Plato once said, "Two trucks having sex, two trucks having sex, my muscles, my muscles, involuntarily flex."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, in that case, I beg pardon — this is definitely a situation where I should have read the rest of the article. I wasn't expecting something like that to have been intentional.

Having now read it, I see what you mean in regards to the dissent. I still (tentatively) feel as if the dissent could make reference to the presumptuousness of the containment procedures — or the procedures could make reference to only 3 populations having been measured or determined to be suitable, rather than claiming that only 3 countries are suitable — because even if the idea is that the Foundation is being arrogant, here, I'm not convinced a lot of readers will catch on. That being said, I can see how you were undermining the premise that the Foundation is, in any sense, in the right.

The anomaly is interesting and unique, and the technical prose is, as expected, extraordinarily proficient — but I'm still leery about an upvote. I feel like this article is well above my 'paygrade' in terms of ethical fiction; I'm not sure how to feel about it. It's well-above 200+, though — so I'm certain my hesitation isn't going to keep anyone up at night.

Regardless, I appreciate you taking the time to respond!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, that's fair.

n.b. that the Ethics Committee dissent literally suggests the prison system as a potential alternative containment strategy, and it's literally the Foundaiton's own comfort with just writing North Korea off which prevents them from taking that option. The phrasing is meant to be callous, because the Foundation is actually in the wrong here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, to start with, let me say that I really, really enjoy pretty much all the stuff you've written (ophite). Your stuff tends to be structurally dense in a way that I really envy, as a writer.

Okay, with that fan-squeeing aside, I do think there's a problem here that's getting missed, and when I started reading this (for the first time, mind you, just now) I was legitimately shocked at the phrasing in the containment procedures. Enough that I immediately stopped reading and came here to post about it.

+ show block
– hide block

As authoritarian-submissive personality traits provide conditional immunity to the signal broadcast by the artifact

Here, we establish that those who exhibit personality traits who are submissive to authority are necessary to contain the anomaly. We then provide some basic metrics for what qualifies (I don't think these metrics are important; even if they're real, your average reader won't know what they mean).

At present, only three national governments possess citizens with conditioning suitable for containment of SCP-1427:

And here we clarify: Only three national governments contain citizens that exhibit the personality traits who are submissive to authority.

I understand what you mean in regards to Stockholm Syndrome and the manner in which people submit to authority merely as a survival mechanism. You're absolutely right; given a choice between having no emotional connections/dying, and forming an emotional connection with our abusers, most of us will (quite understandably) choose to emotionally connect to our abusers. We'll submit, because the alternative is either death or torture (physical, emotional, or both).

But that's not the issue, I think? The issue is how you're (unintentionally, I strenuously add) connecting the idea of submissive personality traits to the populations of three countries with the language here. I get what you're aiming for, I think — this object has to be contained somewhere where people have been repressed, because only a mass of people exhibiting signs of being repressed (specifically, submission to that oppression) can contain it. But I think that, in seeking to accomplish this, you're implying that 1) This sort of dynamic is limited to only these three countries (what about the US prison system?) and 2) The populations of these countries are inherently submissive to authority.

I think that you could phrase the special containment procedures differently to not imply this and still get across what you're saying. Keep in mind, I'm coming in on this cold — I've never looked at this article prior to today, and I actually haven't even read past the containment procedures yet! I know that probably makes it sound like I shouldn't even be writing this post, but I'm hoping that my decision to write this immediately after reading them will give you a sense of how strongly your phrasing came off to me.

I hope none of this comes off as antagonistic or hostile; it's certainly not my intent! I just wanted to throw my two cents in, because I do think a change in language would help avoid what I believe is a completely unintentional implication.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's an uncharitable reading?

The entire purpose of an authoritarian system is to induce its victims to submit, because — absent submission — no authoritarian system even functions. Brutality has to be rationed out: if it isn't, the victims of authoritarianism will fight or flee, and both responses are threats to the system.

I'm not just talking about states. Authoritarian systems include everything from kleptocratic prison-states to actual prisons to abusive marriages, and though we call the state of persistent submission which these systems induce "Stockholm Syndrome," or "prison institutionalization," or "military discipline," or "authoritarian submission," they're all all the same phenomenon. If you have to structure your own thoughts to match some external authority in order to survive, you'll just… do that. I'll do that. Not because humans have a fixed nature, but because humans are flexible when their survival is threatened. Utter submission to authority to save your own life is like a wolf chewing off its own leg to escape a trap.

Now: there's a charitable and uncharitable way to interpret what you're saying. The charitable one is that you're interpreting this as being part of the line of late-19th to midcentury thinking which tries to assign a "national character" to individual countries in an incredibly racist way. Yeah. You could read it that way, and I tried to avoid it, but a couple things about what I actually believe: (a) there's no evidence that attitudes toward authority are fixed at birth, and they're certainly not genetic; (b) within cultures, even highly authoritarian cultures, attitudes differ substantially, except within the ruling class1; and (c) within cultures, attitudes toward authority vary significantly with changes in authority — exactly like you'd predict. There's a lot of political science work on this in particular, and although you can't do survey research inside North Korea, attitudes toward authority vary significantly between the PRC and Taiwan, which were basically the same population until ~70 years ago.

As for the uncharitable interpretation: I don't think you're a tankie, but if you're a tankie who's mistaken a highly stratified kleptocratic monarchy for a meaningfully socialist state, I don't know what to tell you and really don't have any common ground to discuss this with you, other than to point out that literally this entire analytic framework comes out of post-WWII Marxism. Theodor Adorno in particular.

1 TBH, though, I think I'd feel substantially different about whether this was justified if it wasn't literally set in the heart of Pyongyang, where you literally have to be a party member and have formally submitted to state authority in order to live.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As for my thoughts on the article: I liked it then and I like it now, though in retrospect the narrative is a bit clumsy. It tries to tie in the ancient and modern via a single addendum, and while I really like the premise that this thing has been around for ages, it leans too much on exposition.

Still, I like this, more for the material it has inspired/can inspire than the actual article itself - if I remember correctly this is pretty central to an 001 proposal (maybe more than one).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't think of a way to respond to this that won't get me in trouble, and I think you're about to start a very nasty 231-style argument.

I'll just say that what people write and/or upvote doesn't necessarily reflect their beliefs, just its merit as a work of fiction. I wrote an article about a supernatural anti-copyright agency that kidnaps RIAA/MPAA employees, but I don't actually support kidnapping people based on their support for copyright law. As Aristotle said, "It is the mark of an educated mind to be able to entertain a thought without accepting it."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was just reminded of this and IMO it's really wild that a piece whose internal logic premised on a whole populace possessing "authoritarian-submissive personality traits" is at +243 right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. I just love this.

This ties together the prehistoric world, modern day history, and ethics into one package in a way I don't see many other skips do. This is just great. Take my +1, ophite.

item.title: Re: Revision
item.content:encoded:
Nope. There is no canon.

If you like, you can headcanon this as a pre-SCP-2578 version of the documentation, but the author of 1427 is under no obligation to acknowledge the existence of SCP-2578.

item.title: Revision
item.content:encoded: Given that SCP-2578 is throwing SCP-1427 into the Sun shouldn't this skip be updated to reflect that? I guess it's not "Neutralized" atm but it basically is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes.

(This is also, in-fiction, the reason why base-12 systems are very common among early civilizations in the Middle East: they adopted them from Decensus living nearby.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh dang, did the PACG civ use base-12 because descensus (usually) had twelve fingers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've left exactly this comment, or something like it, on three of my SCPs.

"This is not to my taste; please delete immediately" is not likely to be honored.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are nicer ways to word these kind of opinions than "not readable, useless jargon and shouldn't be written or considered". The Criticism Policy specifically states "Don't be an asshole".

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Articles like this shouldn't be written and should not even be considered.

Given the rating of this article, a significant number of readers on the site disagree with you.

Authors are free to write whatever material they wish on the site, so long as it abides by site rules. If the rating remains above deletion threshold, the work will stay on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the worst SCPs I have ever read, it literally does NOTHING since I am not a scientific genius who can understand a pile of useless jargon. Articles like this shouldn't be written and should not even be considered. Downvoted instantly

item.title: Comment and correction
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted this article since it is well written and I like how it ties into 1348. I don't really like the idea of the Foundation supporting North Korea, but the decision to do so is depicted realistically in the article.

I will point out a minor issue, I am not sure if it needs to be corrected but the heights of descensus appear to be Imperial. Here is the height in Metric (rounded): male 2.3m, female 2.2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Question: Do we actually have reason to believe that the citizens of the USSR and the DPRK had/have sufficiently authoritarian personalities, or is that just an assumption based on characteristics of their leadership?

item.title: Re: tag
item.content:encoded: It should, and it has been. Thanks for the watchful eye.

item.title: tag
item.content:encoded: shouldn't this be tagged "K-Scenario"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read and upvoted this a while ago. Re-read now and picked up on a lot more. Everything in the Archaeological report just amazes me and sticks with me long after reading and is just mesmerizing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um……. wow. Every one of your articles I read astounds me even more with their sheer creativity. The only part of that I actually guessed was the obelisk drawing power from all the tests in NK, and I had a few vague inklings about the rest above it. I didn't even think of connecting it to 1348, I guess I need to pay more attention.

With this, officially all all except one of Ophite skips are over 100 net score. So here's to him, for making every single other site member look bad in comparison to him. That bastard, writing good literature for us to enjoy.

Edit: So it turns out Ophite posted two articles since his last edit of his author page. First one's at 108, second one at a mere +27. See kids, this is why it's important to regularly update that page if you're an author and to check with Alexandra if you're trying to post facts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An addition to my (much) earlier post above, I can't help but note that the changing circumstances in real life may change the nature of this article. I'm not sure if this is meant to be more of a snapshot in time kind of deal, but if not, it changes things. For example, Burma is now a (very flawed) democracy, making it unsuitable to contain this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor historical issue: East Germany didn't exist until 1949, so their government couldn't transfer this SCP to the Soviets before then. For this to make sense, you can either push the date back or instead state that the Soviet occupation authorities were the ones who transferred the SCP to the Soviet Union.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
NECRO (though the information in this post may prove useful to anyone else with this question).

Thermonuclear weapons in use today require a primary fission device to create the necessary temperatures for fusion. So yes, the artifact would still interfere with a current day fusion bomb and prevent it from exploding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not much. It screws it up, but — remember — the pulse fails and perseverates against people who have undergone totalitarian conditioning. It'll smack up against a significant portion of the population.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Okay, if this thing launches a pulse every seven nanoseconds, it should infect over 140 million people per second. That means that it could hit the entire population of earth in less than a minute. To what extent does "jamming" the pulse actually decrease this effect?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the US has more advanced weapons… hydrogen bombs anyone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ummm… totalitarian societies are the least efficient… with democracies and republics the things that get through the arguments work until someone gets a law through that stops it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay! +1 for gibborim!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was just about to, but then SCP-1427-ETR-3: ETHICS COMMITTEE DISSENT, SCP-1427 hit it firmly.

The archaeological report makes it firmly SCP material. Better than Zachariah Sitchin

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it a lot. I'm assuming that it isn't feasible to jam the signal being emitted by the artifact, though one of the earlier comments did have a good idea with constructing the containment out of something capable of blocking the signal.

On another note, lots of people who know what they are talking about when it comes to signal and communications. I wonder how many SATCOMers there are on here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This tickles many parts of me, well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is why reading the comment sections is so important, you can find the answers to the questions you didn't even know you had. It has been said by others before me, but I reiterate and reinforce: ophite, you are a true master writer. The only failing I have found in your works is that you give us, the readers, WAY too much credit. Even after being taken by the hand and shown step by step how your story really flows I barely can follow it. I mean this as a compliment, as finding a author who makes me have to think is a glorious thing. As beautiful as it is rare. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tend to have similar uneasy feelings when any fantasy rubs against actual human totalitarianism— say various stories where the Holocaust was an exercise in black magic/human sacrifice. Where I draw the line is at the question: "Does the element of fiction actually excuse or absolve the human actors of their moral responsibility?" Basically, if the Nazis were trying to summon Azathoth, it doesn't cross the line because the fictional Nazis still have responsibility for their acts. OTOH if Azatoth was possessing the Nazis, it would cross the line because it's now all Azathoth's fault.

So, by my own rule of thumb, this passes because the situation in North Korea isn't caused by the SCP, the situation just offers a convenient method of containment.

Edit: Just noticed the year of the OP, sorry for the necropost.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A WINNAR IS YOU. In any case, since you found the easter egg backstory, some stuff you might not pick up on:

(1) There have been small populations of decensus on Earth since roughly two million years ago. They naturally clustered in small populations around the various inner sanctums of the Elohim, who had apparently uplifted or created them.

Human development toward sapiens is the result of interbreeding breeding between Homo sapiens decensus and Homo erectus in an attempt to raise the naturally miserable fertility rate of decensus — there were never more than a few tens of thousands of them on Earth at the same time, and you look at the archaeological record in 1427, you'll see a lot of decensus infant remains in their midden. Insofar as they had a relationship with decensus at all, many early sapiens were unskilled laborers around decensus settlements. But there were a lot of wild sapiens around, which were mostly ignored by decensus, and didn' t compete with them for resources (largely because there were so few left.)

Decensus technology was heavily based on the Elohim they served: they used their gods as energy sources, among other things. Devices like SCP-1427 were multifunction defensive perimeters: they were used to keep wild populations of sapiens away and servant populations of sapiens servile. They powered themselves by drawing energy off of nearby radioactivity; originally, an Elohim sanctum, but they can technically pull power off of any available source within a very large range. Right now, the 1427 obelisk is drawing power from North Korea's nuclear program — which is why their bombs keep mysteriously fizzling — but in the past, it powered itself up from a Russian nuclear power plant, a nuclear bomb, and Chernobyl NPP (eventually resulting in the meltdown).

The reasons the Germans could never get it to work is that they didn't have a suitable power source within range. Conversely, if 1427 were moved out of the range of something it could draw power from, it would shut down. But the Foundation hasn't discovered that yet, mostly because it can only hire unimaginative fascist drones to work on studying it.

(2) It is very unclear what the Elohim actually are: they are something like gods, and are of extraordinary but undefined power, but have very short attention spans and very little perspective on the world as humans see it. They seem to be more concerned with lineages than individuals, and more concerned with patterns than events. When the inhabitant of 1348 confused Dr. Kahn for his father, this was a reflection of its strange myopia about the world: it did not know that Kahn considered himself to be a different entity than his ancestors.

SCP-1348 is the last remaining sanctum for a member of the Elohim. The entity inside, Yehom, seems to have betrayed its brethren in order to save Homo sapiens, and was grievously wounded in some undefined way in defending humanity. When the beryllium bronze sheath lifts, the other Elohim can remember that it exists, and that puts it in danger. None of the Abrahamic religions' apocalypses can occur until the rest of the Elohim murder Yehom. (Presumably, then, the Elohim would turn on humanity and end them as well.)

The rest of the 1348-like inner sanctums were destroyed by sapiens populations. The rectangular divot in the Tamarranset Ossuary is, in fact, the remains of an Elohim inner sanctum like the one which Yehom inhabits. This appears not to have killed the Elohim which once lived there.

(3) SCP-1348-1-E is a different sort of servitor of the Elohim; it was the last of its type, and is now extinct. They may have been purpose-built from Elohim infants, or may have been a different sort of being entirely. They survived entirely on the deadly neutron radiation emitted by the Elohim, in the same way that PACG technology was powered, and had only vestigial digestive tracts. So when the wounded seraph was removed from the Sanctum, it died of malnutrition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to craft theories about SCP back stories and mysteries. I'm very often wrong and it's nice to at least be close for once. I guess if you throw enough darts at the board, you are bound to hit the mark sooner or later. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I forget parts of it, but yeah, this is pretty good guesswork.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this at all close to said backstory? Or am I pissing in the wind here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having heard ophite's backstory for this one, I assure you, reading too much into it would be incredibly difficult.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Decensus n. The process of descending or falling from a higher position.

Homo Sapiens Decensus could refer to two scenarios. The first and most obvious would be that they fell from power through destruction of their civilization and allowed Homo Sapiens Sapiens to rise. The second scenario is that they originated from a higher position (plane, dimension?) and came to earth. Which when combined with their physical traits (Gigantism, polydactyly) and superior technology puts me in the mind of something like the Nephilim or Elioud. This would jibe well with SCP-1348 and the implication that Humanity was a mistake. The thought being that the PACG were the chosen of the Entities referenced in SCP-1348 and Earth was meant for them. Perhaps they destroyed themselves or maybe proto-humanity was somehow responsible. Either way Yehom protected and guided Humanity to become the dominant species. A position that was never meant for them. A act which He is currently suffering for.

Or maybe I read into stuff too much. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think I can help you refine it any further, because you're one of the authors I base my own tone off of, and you would know better than me wrong vs right tone. It's hard to quantify exactly what I like about your writing, but it never falters on concision or objectivity. I always feel like I'm reading from a person who really knows exactly what they're talking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, I've heard that before, so I'm apparently doing something right. Could you be more specific about what you think I'm doing right? It's… sometimes hard to see from the inside, and I'd like to refine what I'm doing to the extent that it's possible.
